title: Compositional Embeddings Using Complementary Partitions for Memory-Efficient Recommendation Systems (Quotient-Remainder trick)
authors: Hao-Jun Michael Shi (Northwestern University), Dheevatsa Mudigere, Maxim Naumov, Jiyan Yang (Facebook)
tokenization_method: Compositional embeddings: instead of one giant lookup table W in R^{|S|xD} per categorical feature (one row per category), a category's index i is decomposed into multiple sub-indices via 'complementary partitions' of the category set, each sub-index looks up a SMALL embedding table, and the small embeddings are combined by an operation omega into the final D-dim embedding. The canonical instance is the QUOTIENT-REMAINDER (Q-R) trick: pick a bucket count m, look up table W1 in R^{mxD} at j = i mod m (remainder) and table W2 in R^{(|S|/m)xD} at k = i \ m (integer quotient), then combine, by default x_emb = x_rem (elementwise-mult) x_quo. Because remainder and quotient together uniquely determine i (they are COMPLEMENTARY partitions: any two distinct categories differ on at least one partition), the composed embedding is unique per category WITHOUT storing |S| rows. Generalizes to k partitions (generalized Q-R, Chinese-Remainder partitions) giving a tuple of k small-table lookups combined into one vector. A 'path-based' variant instead seeds one embedding from partition 1 then routes it through a sequence of per-partition learned transforms (linear / MLP) M_{k,p_k(x)} o ... o M_{2,p_2(x)}. Partitions here are FIXED modular-arithmetic codes (a fixed codebook), not learned/stored discrete codes, so unlike product-quantization there is no O(|S|) code storage.
feature_types_handled: Categorical only (high-cardinality sparse/nominal categorical features in CTR/recsys). Dense numerical features are handled outside the trick (log-transformed and fed to the bottom MLP in the experiments). No temporal modeling, no missing-value typing (unlabeled categoricals just mapped to NULL). It is a memory-compression scheme for the categorical-embedding-table half of a model, not a general heterogeneous-feature tokenizer.
token_granularity: Per-feature embedding produced from a multi-index decomposition. One categorical VALUE is represented not by one row but by a TUPLE of k small-table rows (the complementary-partition codes), combined into a single per-feature embedding vector. So internally it is a learned-code / multi-lookup scheme, but the unit consumed downstream is still one embedding per categorical feature (not a multi-token sequence as in TIGER's semantic IDs).
numerical_strategy: None proposed. The 13 Criteo dense features are log-transformed and consumed by the network's dense/bottom MLP; the trick never touches continuous features.
categorical_strategy: Replace the single per-feature embedding table with multiple smaller tables indexed by complementary partitions (remainder, quotient, or generalized modular codes), combined by an operation (concatenation / addition / elementwise multiplication) or by a learned path of transforms. Elementwise multiplication is reported as the most scalable/effective operation overall. Thresholding is recommended: only compress tables whose cardinality exceeds a threshold, leave small tables full.
high_cardinality_handling: This is the core contribution. For a feature with |S| ~ 10^7 categories, the full table needs O(|S|D) (multiple GB). The Q-R trick stores only O(|S|/m D + mD); the k-partition generalization reaches an optimal O(k |S|^{1/k} D) (e.g. two equal partitions -> O(sqrt(|S|) D)). Crucially it preserves a UNIQUE embedding per category (Theorem 1, proved for concatenation), unlike the plain hashing/remainder trick which collapses many categories onto one row. It does NOT use category frequency or learned semantic structure -- partitions are arbitrary modular buckets.
temporal_handling: None. No time axis, recurrence, patching, or static/time-varying fusion. Purely a per-category embedding-table compression.
scales_to_many_features: Scales in CATEGORY CARDINALITY per feature (|S| -> |S|^{1/k}), not in the NUMBER of heterogeneous features. The Criteo setup has 26 categorical features; the trick is applied per-table independently with a chosen #collisions/threshold. It bounds the parameter cost of high-cardinality embedding tables, which is the dominant memory term in DLRM, but it does nothing about sequence length, attention cost, or feature-interaction cost for transformer-style tokenization. Reported network-level: baseline full tables ~5.4x10^8 params; up to ~15x smaller model (60 collisions) at similar quality to 4-collision hashing.
downstream_model: Modified Deep & Cross Network (DCN: 3-hidden-layer deep net 512/256/64 + 6-layer cross network) and Facebook DLRM (bottom MLP 512/256/64, top MLP 512/256, pairwise-dot interaction). Embedding dim 16 (32 for non-compositional concatenation when thresholding). These are MLP/cross-net CTR models, NOT transformers.
pretraining_objective: None. Trained fully end-to-end with the rest of the network (single epoch, batch 128, Adagrad/AMSGrad, binary cross-entropy on Criteo). No self-supervised or target-aware code learning; the partition codes are fixed (modular arithmetic), so no code-learning objective is needed and no codes are stored.
key_results: Criteo Kaggle Display Advertising (45M rows, 13 dense + 26 categorical; 6 days train, day-7 split val/test). Q-R trick interpolates between hashing-trick compression and full-table accuracy (Fig 4, 4x reduction). Best operation = elementwise multiplication overall (best for DCN; concatenation best for DLRM under thresholding). Vs hashing with 4 collisions, matched-or-better quality reached at up to 60 collisions => ~15x smaller model. At 4 collisions: within 0.3% of full-table baseline (DCN) and 0.7% (DLRM); thresholding improved DLRM gap from 0.7% to 0.5% error while keeping ~4x reduction. Path-based (MLP transforms, 4 collisions): best hidden size 64 (DCN test loss 0.45252; DLRM 0.45306) but path-based did NOT beat operation-based embeddings in these preliminary runs. Single table reported (Table 1).
baselines_compared: Hashing trick (remainder-only feature hashing) as the primary compression baseline; full embedding tables as the accuracy upper bound; 'feature generation' (use each partition's lookup as a separate sparse feature) as an alternative that adds ~0.5M extra params. Operations compared head-to-head: Concat / Add / Mult / Feature / Hash / Full. Related work contrasted: product quantization and learned discrete-code embeddings (Chen et al., Shu & Nakayama), Tensor-Train embedding compression (Khrulkov et al.) -- argued to require O(|S|) code storage or to reduce D rather than |S|.
transferable_idea: Bound the memory of HIGH-CARDINALITY CATEGORICAL embedding tables by replacing one |S|-row table with a small set of complementary-partition tables (e.g. Q-R: a sqrt(|S|)-ish quotient table and an m-row remainder table) combined by elementwise multiplication, guaranteeing a unique, collision-free embedding per category at O(k|S|^{1/k}D) instead of O(|S|D) -- a fixed-codebook scheme that trains end-to-end and stores NO explicit codes. For the 70+-feature setting this is the cheapest, simplest way to keep the categorical half of the feature set's embedding tables from dominating memory when several fields are very high cardinality, applied per-field above a cardinality threshold.
limitations: Authors state: (1) partitions ignore category FREQUENCY and learn no intrinsic semantic structure -- arbitrary modular buckets, so two unrelated categories can share a quotient/remainder code (uniqueness of the COMPOSED vector is preserved, but the shared sub-embeddings still entangle unrelated categories). (2) Q-R enforces arbitrary structure -> arbitrary, monotone-increasing loss as compression increases; quality depends on how well partitions happen to reflect category structure (Criteo carries no such side knowledge). (3) Path-based variant adds non-embedding params and was harder to train; did not beat operation-based in preliminary runs. (4) The optimal operation is architecture-dependent (mult for DCN, concat for DLRM) -- no universal choice. (5) Codebook-learning methods (which DO use structure) were argued impractical at recsys cardinalities, so the trick deliberately trades structure for storage.
pitfalls: 1) SCOPE: this is a categorical-embedding COMPRESSION trick, NOT a tokenizer. It produces no tokens for numerical/temporal features and adds no representational power -- at best it matches full-table quality, never exceeds it. Citing it as a 'tokenization method' for 70+ mixed features overreaches; its job is purely to cap categorical memory. 2) Results are CTR-loss on Criteo with MLP/cross-net models; they say nothing about transformer-tokenizer accuracy or about numerical/temporal features. 3) Elementwise-multiplication of independently-initialized sub-embeddings can hurt optimization/scale (the paper notes AMSGrad >> Adagrad specifically for mult) -- naive adoption may train poorly without the right optimizer. 4) Uniqueness theorem is proved for CONCATENATION; for mult/add uniqueness is not guaranteed in general (multiplication can map distinct tuples to equal products if zeros/sign patterns coincide), a subtlety to verify before relying on collision-freeness. 5) 'O(sqrt(|S|))' is an asymptotic best case requiring balanced partitions; with thresholding and per-field tuning the realized savings are smaller and feature-dependent. 6) Compared against the HASHING trick, not against learned/semantic codes -- so 'beats baseline' means 'beats blind hashing', a low bar vs modern semantic-ID/RQ-VAE codebooks that also add structure.
relevance_to_topic: Medium-narrow. It is the canonical, production-deployed (in Meta's open-source DLRM) way to keep high-cardinality categorical embedding tables from being the memory bottleneck -- directly useful for the categorical half of a 70+-heterogeneous-feature set, but contributes nothing to numerical, temporal, or feature-interaction tokenization and never improves accuracy beyond a full table.
code_available: yes -- incorporated into the official Facebook/Meta DLRM repo (https://github.com/facebookresearch/dlrm); the Q-R trick is a small, standalone, easily-reimplemented embedding layer.
